content.problemStatement:
Eat, drink, and be scary

There are NNN spooky days left until Halloween.

Dracula dines at a mysterious restaurant that changes its spooky menu daily. He particularly enjoys what they serve on Tuesday.

Today is Monday, so he wishes to calculate how many times he can indulge in his favourite menu in the next NNN days **(including today)** before Halloween.

Note that Dracula follows the standard 777-day calendar, with Tuesday immediately following Monday.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The only line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, denoting the number of spooky days.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of times Dracula would have had his favorite meal after NNN days.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N, the number of days (including today).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line with the number of Tuesdays that occur in the next N days (including today).

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=1 there is no Tuesday. For N=10 Tuesdays are on days 2 and 9 → 2 times. For N=15 the same two Tuesdays appear. For N=16 an additional Tuesday appears on day 16, giving 3.
N=2 → Tuesday on day 2. N=7 → only day 2 is Tuesday. N=14 → Tuesdays on days 2 and 9.
N=20 → Tuesdays on days 2,9,16. N=1000 → floor((1000+5)/7)=143 Tuesdays.

solution.approach: Day 1 is Monday, so day 2 is Tuesday. The pattern repeats every 7 days. Therefore a day i (1-indexed) is a Tuesday iff i % 7 == 2. The count of such i in [1, N] is floor((N - 2) / 7) + 1 for N >= 2, otherwise 0. This simplifies to floor((N + 5) / 7). For each test case we compute ans = (N + 5) / 7 using integer division.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
